fuzzy_prd: We need a reusable documentation toolkit that our content teams can use to convert raw web pages and generated text into clean, ready-to-store knowledge packs. The basic idea is: given a web page (in raw HTML), the tool should produce a clean text version in a standard lightweight markup format so we can store everything uniformly. On top of that, our AI-generated drafts often come back wrapped in extra formatting artifacts or with metadata headers that shouldn't be in the final stored copy — we need something that strips all that away and ensures the output always ends cleanly. We also want to support assembling a knowledge pack from a declared list of sources, where each source's content is gathered into one combined buffer. This step has to be strict: if any source turns out to be insecure or unreachable, the whole operation should fail immediately instead of producing partial results — similar to how the auth module handles credential validation failures. Finally, each knowledge pack is described by a small configuration object that gives it a name, a short summary, an optional extra-guidance field, and an ordered list of content sources; we need a parser that reads this structure and surfaces all fields cleanly. Everything should be organized so the core logic is completely decoupled from command routing and input/output handling, just like the way our data pipeline separates processing stages.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Each heading level (h1-h6) is rendered as a line with the matching number of hash characters followed by a space and the heading text. The rendered block is surrounded by newlines: the format is '
# content

' (with a leading newline and a trailing double-newline). After trimming the overall result, adjacent headings produce output like '#### H4


##### H5


###### H6
'.

Both ordered (ol) and unordered (ul) lists render identically as flat bullet lists. Every list item (li) becomes a line starting with '- ' followed by the item text. The ol/ul container wraps the items with a leading and trailing newline: '\n<items>\n'. There is no numbering; ordered lists are NOT rendered with numbers.

A video element without a title attribute uses the default label 'Video'. An iframe element without a title attribute uses the default label 'Iframe'. A video with a poster attribute renders as a clickable image: '[![title](poster)](videoUrl)'. Without a poster it renders as a plain link: '[title](videoUrl)'.

The cleanContent function strips an outer fenced code block only when the entire content is wrapped in one. It matches a regex '^\s*```[a-zA-Z]*\s*
' (case-insensitive) at the start. The closing fence is matched by '
```\s*$'. Internal code fences belonging to real content are preserved intact. After stripping the fence, front-matter (bracketed by '---' lines) is also removed. The final result is trimmed and always ends with exactly one trailing newline ('
').

Front-matter removal searches for the first occurrence of '---'. If it is at index 0 (start of string), it looks for the closing '---' starting from index 3 and strips everything up to and including that closing delimiter plus 3 characters. If the first '---' occurs after index 0 (noise before it), it still tries to find a matching closing '---' and strips from the first occurrence through the closing one. After stripping, the result is trimmed. This means leading noise before a front-matter block is also removed.

Each resource's content in the aggregated buffer is introduced by a header line of the exact form '--- Raw content from <resource> ---' (using writeln, so followed by a newline). For local files the content is appended verbatim (also using writeln, adding one trailing newline). For HTTPS URLs the content is first converted to Markdown via MarkdownConverter before being appended. The output is the concatenation of all these header+content pairs with no additional separators.

When a resource is a relative path (not starting with 'http://' or 'https://') and configDir is null (has_config_dir is false), the error category is 'missing_config_dir'. When the relative path does not resolve to an existing file, the error category is 'local_file_not_found'. When a resource starts with 'http://', the error is 'insecure_url'. The error output format is exactly: 'error=<category>\nresource=<resource>\n'.

When the config map omits the 'instructions' key, the output line is 'instructions=<none>' (the literal string '<none>', not an empty string). When present, it outputs 'instructions=<value>'. Resources are output as zero-indexed lines: 'resource[0]=...', 'resource[1]=...', etc. The resource_count line uses the exact format 'resource_count=<n>'.

A 'details' element is rendered as raw HTML with converted inner content. The format is '
<details>
<converted_inner_content>
</details>
'. The 'summary' child is rendered as '<summary><content></summary>' verbatim (not converted to Markdown). The inner body text nodes and non-summary children are converted normally. The final result after trimming the overall output ends with a single newline.

The table divider row between header and body uses '---|' per column with no leading space: '|---|---|'. Each data cell is rendered as ' <trimmed_cell_text> |'. The header row starts with '|'. When no explicit thead is present, the first row is promoted to be the header and removed from the body rows. The table output starts with a leading '
' and ends with '

'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how the auth module handles credential validation failures
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The ResourceFetcherService.fetchAndConvertContent method throws an Exception immediately on the first validation failure (insecure URL, missing config directory, or missing local file), preventing partial resource aggregation — mirroring a fail-fast validation pattern.

context_pointers.vague_reference: just like the way our data pipeline separates processing stages
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The architecture enforces Separation of Concerns: the dispatcher (rcb_tests/dispatcher/dispatcher.dart) is a thin adapter that routes JSON actions to core service classes (MarkdownConverter, GeminiService.cleanContent, ResourceFetcherService, SkillParams.fromJson) with no business logic in the I/O layer.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That question does not match any documented constraint. Please consult the start.md specification or inspect the golden source files for authoritative behavior details.

feature1_1_headings.json.description: Convert HTML heading elements of all six levels into their Markdown equivalents. Each heading level maps to a line beginning with the matching number of hash characters followed by a space and the heading text. The input is an object carrying the raw HTML string to convert; the output is the rendered Markdown text.

feature1_2_inline_emphasis.json.description: Convert inline text-emphasis HTML elements into Markdown. Strong-emphasis elements become text wrapped in double asterisks, ordinary-emphasis elements become text wrapped in single asterisks, and strike-through elements become text wrapped in double tildes. Both spellings of each emphasis kind are accepted. The input carries the raw HTML string; the output is the rendered Markdown.

feature1_3_code.json.description: Convert HTML code markup into Markdown. Inline code spans become text wrapped in single backticks, while preformatted blocks become fenced code blocks delimited by triple-backtick lines with the literal text placed on its own lines between the fences. The input carries the raw HTML string; the output is the rendered Markdown.

feature1_4_block_structure.json.description: Convert block-level and structural HTML into Markdown. Paragraphs render their text followed by a blank line; block quotes render the text prefixed with a greater-than marker; a thematic-break element renders as a horizontal rule of three dashes; a hard line-break element renders as a newline within the surrounding text; and generic structural containers (such as a division, a section, a main region, or an article) simply pass their inner content through, including any nested inline formatting. The input carries the raw HTML string; the output is the rendered Markdown.

feature1_5_lists.json.description: Convert HTML list markup into Markdown. Both unordered and ordered lists are rendered as a flat bullet list: every list item becomes a line introduced by a dash and a space followed by the item text. The input carries the raw HTML string; the output is the rendered Markdown.

feature1_6_links_media.json.description: Convert HTML hyperlinks and embedded-media elements into Markdown. A hyperlink becomes the bracketed link text followed by the destination URL in parentheses. An image becomes an image reference: an exclamation mark, the alternative text in brackets (empty when absent), then the source URL in parentheses. A video becomes a link whose label is the supplied title (or a default label) and whose target is the direct source URL or the URL of a nested source element; when a poster image is supplied the video becomes a clickable poster image linking to the video. An inline-frame element becomes a link using its title (or a default label) and its source URL. The input carries the raw HTML string; the output is the rendered Markdown.

feature1_7_tables.json.description: Convert an HTML table into a Markdown pipe table. The header cells render as a pipe-delimited row, followed by a divider row of dashed segments (one per column), followed by one pipe-delimited row per body row. Header cells may come from an explicit header section; when none is present the first row is promoted to act as the header. Cell text is trimmed of surrounding whitespace. The input carries the raw HTML string; the output is the rendered Markdown.

feature1_8_definition_lists.json.description: Convert an HTML definition list into Markdown. Each term renders as bold text on its own line, and each associated definition renders on its own line introduced by a colon and a space. Multiple term/definition pairs are rendered in order. The input carries the raw HTML string; the output is the rendered Markdown.

feature1_9_collapsible.json.description: Convert an HTML collapsible-disclosure element into output that preserves the disclosure and summary tags verbatim (because many Markdown renderers support raw HTML for collapsible sections) while converting the inner body content to Markdown. The summary caption is wrapped in its original tag, and the surrounding disclosure tags bracket the converted content. The input carries the raw HTML string; the output is the rendered result.

feature2_clean_content.json.description: Clean up raw generated text so it is ready to be embedded as document content. If the text is wrapped in a single fenced code block (a fence line optionally tagged with a language, possibly with surrounding whitespace and in any letter case), the outer fence is stripped. If the text begins with — or contains near its start — a delimited front-matter block (a section bracketed by triple-dash lines), everything up to and including that block is removed. Any internal code blocks belonging to the real content are left intact. The result is trimmed and guaranteed to end with exactly one trailing newline. The input carries the raw text string; the output is the cleaned text.

input.content:
```markdown
---
key: value
---
# Title
Content with code:
```dart
print('hello');
```
```

feature3_resource_fetching.json.description: Fetch a list of source resources and aggregate their textual content into a single buffer, failing fast on any problem so downstream processing is never fed partial or invalid material. Each resource that is a relative path is read from a provided base directory and appended verbatim; each appended resource is introduced by a header line naming the resource. Validation is strict: a plain (non-secure) URL is rejected outright; a relative path requested without any base directory is rejected; and a relative path that does not resolve to an existing file is rejected. Rejections surface as a neutral error category together with the offending resource identifier. The input carries the list of resources, whether a base directory is available, and the in-memory contents of any local files keyed by their relative name; the output is either the aggregated buffer or the normalized error.

cases.expected_output:
--- Raw content from local_doc.md ---
# Local Doc
content

feature4_config_parsing.json.description: Parse a skill definition from a configuration map and expose its fields. A definition always carries a name, a description, and a list of resource identifiers; it may optionally carry an instructions string, which is absent when the configuration omits it. The input carries the configuration map; the output reports each parsed field, including the resource count and the resources in order, with the instructions field shown as absent when not provided.

cases.expected_output:
name=test-skill
description=Test Description
instructions=<none>
resource_count=1
resource[0]=http://example.com

name=test-skill
description=Test Description
instructions=Do not hallucinate.
resource_count=1
resource[0]=http://example.com
